EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A complex and slow-moving system brought an extended period of snow to central Illinois from December 25th through December 27th.  The first wave of significant snow occurred during the night of December 25th through midday on the 26th.  Due to favorable temperature and moisture conditions aloft, very large snow flakes were produced, resulting in impressive snowfall rates.  By the time the wave departed the area by the late afternoon of December 26th, a wide swath of 3 to 6 inch snow accumulations had occurred along and west of the Illinois River. A weaker wave brought another period of light snow during the morning of December 27th.  By the time the precipitation ended, all of central Illinois had picked up between 3 and 5 inches of snow, with 7 to 9 inch amounts common along and west of the Illinois River.  Due to the prolonged period of snow, numerous traffic accidents occurred around the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Numerous traffic accidents were reported across Douglas County due to snow-covered roadways.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A fast-moving upper-level disturbance tracked through central Illinois on December 19, 2009, bringing a period of light snow to the area.  Total snowfall was generally around 1 inch...with higher amounts northwest of the Illinois River.  Due to the light accumulation of snow, many area roads became slick and hazardous.  As a result, a few traffic accidents were reported.  The most serious accident involved a car on I-55 in Springfield that skidded off the road and hit a light post, killing the driver.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Due to icy road conditions, a car skidded off I-55 in Springfield near the Sangamon Avenue exit at 6:10 AM.  The car struck a light post, killing the driver.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A small but potent winter storm moved from the plains across northern Illinois during the morning hours of January 14th.  Air temperatures were only 5 to 15 degrees above zero and snow/water ratios were very high, in some cases 40 to 1.  Snow begain falling just after midnight on January 14th and ended by early afternoon.  Stormtotal snowfall amounts included 7.2 inches in Morris, 7.0 inches in Dixon, 7.0 inches in Kankakee, 7.0 inches in Joliet, 6.5 inches in Peotone, 6.5 inches in Yorkville, 6.5 inches in Dwight, 6.0 inches in Oregon and 5.7 inches in Homewood.

A slow moving winter storm moved across northern Illinois on December 26th.  Snow began to fall during the early morning hours and continued through the late evening hours.  The snow was heavy at times in some areas and the snow to water ratio was quite large with the driest snow reported to be 40 to 1.  A foot of snow fell across the northwest suburbs of Chicago with a large area receiving six inches or more.  Snowfall totals included 12.1 inches in St. Charles; 12.0 inches in Elburn; 12.0 inches in North Aurora; 12.0 inches 1 mile north of Carol Stream; 11.9 inches 7 miles north northwest of St. Charles; 11.1 inches in Batavia; 10.6 inches in Streamwood; 10.3 inches in Elgin; 10.0 inches in Woodstock; 9.4 inches 4 miles north northwest of Rockford; 9.2 inches 3 miles southwest of Barrington; 8.9 inches in Mundelein; 7.5 inches in Island Lake; 6.7 inches in Downers Grove; 6.2 inches in Roscoe; 6.1 inches in Rockford and 6.0 inches at Chicago O'Hare Airport.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain fell across parts of west central Will County during the afternoon and early evening of June 16th.  Storms developed over the same areas as they slowly moved northeast, producing torrential rains over a relatively small area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain fell across parts west central Will County causing flash flooding.  Numerous roads were flooded and closed.  In Joliet, Hammas was closed between Jefferson and Onedia; Midhurst Lane at Midhurst Court was flooded with water two feet deep; Edgerton was flooded between Midhurst Lane and Westfield and also north of Harrogate Avenue.  In Crest Hill, Route 30 at Larkin was flooded with stalled vehicles in the intersection and the intersection of Arduagh and Noonan Drive was flooded.  In Romeoville, ten inches of flowing was reported along Weber Road near Interstate 55 and Romeo Road was heavily flooded.  Basement flooding was also reported.  Storm total rainfall amounts included 4.09 inches just southwest of Larkin and Theodore in Joliet; 3.95 inches just northwest of Gaylord and Theodore in Joliet; 3.05 inches at the National Weather Service office in Romeoville; 2.50 inches near Bruce and Lawrence Avenue in Lockport; 2.28 inches was reported in Homer Glen; 2.27 inches at the Joliet Lock & Dam; 1.74 inches in Plainfield and 1.70 inches in Shorewood.

A strong cold front moved through the region triggering showers and thunderstorms. Damaging winds, large hail and tornadoes were reported with the storms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tornado touched down about a quarter of a mile north of Rosedale.  The roof of a machine shed was lifted off and tossed 50 to 75 yards to the north northeast.  The tornado then skipped along and north of Fieldon Rosedale Road.  There was mainly large limb and isolated tree damage along this path.  One area of large tree damage was located southwest of the intersection of Fieldon Rosedale Road and Otter Creek Road.  A water pump house was nearly destroyed at this location before the tornado lifted and dissipated.

Low pressure tracked from Missouri across north-central Illinois on April 5th.  Scattered thunderstorms developed in advance of this feature...with a few of the storms producing marginally severe hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Nickel-sized hail lasted for around 5 minutes and covered 80 to 90 percent of the ground.

A cold front moved into central Illinois during the afternoon and evening of April 25th, triggering isolated strong thunderstorms.  A few of the storms produced marginally severe hail across parts of Tazewell and Woodford counties.

A cold front moved into central Illinois during the afternoon and evening of April 25th, triggering isolated strong thunderstorms.  A few of the storms produced marginally severe hail across parts of Tazewell and Woodford counties.

A warm front lifting northward into central Illinois triggered widespread showers and thunderstorms across the area on April 30th.  1 to 2 inches of rain falling on already saturated soils led to minor river flooding in a few spots.  In particular, the Little Wabash River overflowed its banks in Effingham County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Little Wabash River overflowed its banks and spilled across West Fayette Road between Effingham and Lake Sara.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered thunderstorms during the early morning hours of April 25th created lightning which damaged the water treatment plant in Petersburg.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Lightning struck the water treatment plant in Petersburg. The lightning damaged the automatic control system and security system. There were no injuries and water service was not disrupted.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms developed during the afternoon hours of April 20th producing multiple reports of pea size hail with one report of nickel size hail in Peru.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Penny to nickel size hail was reported at the intersection of 4th Street and Cross Street in Peru.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms developed during the afternoon hours of April 25th producing a few reports of pea size hail with one report of penny size hail in Sycamore.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Marble to penny size hail was reported near Route 23 and Bethany Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms developed during the morning hours of April 26th producing heavy rainfall and some flooding along with one report of nickel size hail in Elgin.
Thunderstorms developed during the morning hours of April 26th producing heavy rainfall and some flooding along with one report of nickel size hail in Elgin.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Minor flooding was reported in Carol Stream after 1.57 inches of rainfall was recorded.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain fell across parts of northern and central Illinois during the afternoon and evening of April 27th causing minor flooding in some areas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flooding was reported in low lying areas and on some streets.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain fell across parts of northern and central Illinois during the afternoon and evening of April 27th causing minor flooding in some areas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flooding was reported on some streets and in poorly drained areas.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain fell across parts of northern and central Illinois during the afternoon and evening of April 27th causing minor flooding in some areas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Minor flooding of streets and underpasses was reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain fell across parts of northern and central Illinois during the afternoon and evening of April 27th causing minor flooding in some areas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Minor flooding was reported in Streator after 1.52 inches of rainfall.  A creek was reported overflowing its banks behind a house.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong to severe thunderstorms moved across northern Illinois during the morning and afternoon hours of March 8th.  Heavy rain and flooding were also reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Up to six inches of standing water was reported near Route 53 and Caton Farm Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong winds gusted as high as 50 mph during the late evening of March 10th and early morning on March 11th.  Tree limbs and power lines were blown down in some areas.  Measured wind gusts included 48 mph in Lansing; 47 mph in Kankakee; 46 mph in Lynwood; 45 mph in Aurora and 45 mph in Peru.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Tree limbs and power lines were blown down.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong to severe storms moved across north central Illinois during the late evening hours of March 24th into the early morning hours of March 25th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: One airplane was flipped over and five other airplanes were damaged at Chicago Executive Airport.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A winter storm moved northeast across northern Illinois on March 28th and 29th, producing heavy wet snow which accumulated several inches.  Stormtotal snowfall amounts include 7.0 inches in Gurnee, 7.0 inches 3 miles west northwest of Bull Valley, 7.0 inches in Lindenhurst, 6.5 inches in Algonquin and 5.5 inches in Waukegan.
Heavy rains of 1.5 to 3 inches fell across parts of west central Illinois during the early morning hours of April 30 resulting in some flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rains of 1 to 3 inches resulted in the flooding of Camp Creek from near Colusa, IL to the Dallas City, IL area during the morning of April 30.  The creek was well out of its banks and flooded low-lying areas in the vicinity.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Heavy rains of 1.5 to 3 inches fell across parts of west central Illinois during the early morning hours of April 30 resulting in some flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Rainfall of 2.40 inches was recorded about 4 miles south southwest of Seaton, IL the morning of April 30.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Heavy rains of 1.5 to 3 inches fell across parts of west central Illinois during the early morning hours of April 30 resulting in some flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rains of 2 to 3 inches fell over portions of northeast Henderson County during the morning of April 30 resulting in flooding of creeks, streams, and low-lying areas as well as some basements of area residences.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong upper level disturbance and cold air aloft produced some instability showers and thunderstorms during the morning of April 5.  Some of the stronger showers and storms produced hail up to three quarters of an inch in diameter.

Heavy rains on March 24th resulted in the Rock River at Joslin and Moline going above their respective moderate flood stage levels during the last week of March.  They were still in moderate flooding at the beginning of April.  The stage at Joslin fell below its 14 foot moderate flood stage level around midday on April 5.  The stage at Moline fell below its 13 foot moderate flood stage level around midday on April 4.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rains on March 24th resulted in the Rock River at Joslin and Moline going above their respective moderate flood stage levels during the last week of March. In Carol Stream, Klein Creek overflowed its banks with street flooding reported.  In Wheaton, Main Street was flooded north of Route 38.

Visibility in those areas was one-quarter mile or less. A light accretion of ice was observed, mostly on elevated surfaces such as vehicles.

A strong cold front moved through the region triggering showers and thunderstorms. Damaging winds, large hail and tornadoes were reported with the storms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tornado touched down about 3.5 miles south southwest of Beaucoup and travelled to the northeast.  It blew down several trees and snapped numerous tree limbs along its path before lifting and dissipating about 3 miles south of Beaucoup.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front moved through the region triggering showers and thunderstorms. Damaging winds, large hail and tornadoes were reported with the storms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The tornado touched down on County Road 51, about three quarters of a mile west southwest of the intersection of Roodhouse Road and 2000E.  A large machine shed was totally destroyed at this location.  The tornado travelled northeast damaging a number of large trees between CR 51 and 2000E.  The tornado crossed 2000E and damaged a large number of trees east of 2000E.  The damage width at this location was two tenths of a mile.  Further to the northeast, the tornado damaged a church at the intersection of Roodhouse Road and County Road 2075E.  The entire church was displaced about 10 feet.  A machine shed south of the church was destroyed with debris extending to the north towards the church.  Many trees were damaged in this area.  The tornado crossed Roodhouse Road and travelled just south of a farmstead on CR 2100E.  There was minor shingle damage to the home, one machine shed was damaged.  A second farm sustained damage on CR 2600N, about a quarter of a mile west of Illinois Route 267.  At this location, one barn and three machine sheds sustained severe damage while parts of the roof and sides of the farm house were uplifted and displaced to the east.  The damage path width was a tenth of a mile at this location.  Debris from these structures were tossed over 120 yards to the east.  The tornado crossed Illinois Route 267 and travelled over an open field then crossed CR 298 where tree damage occurred.  The tornado severely damaged a large section of mature pine trees just south of CR 304 and CR 2700N in an open field before it lifted and dissipated.

A strong cold front moved through the region triggering showers and thunderstorms. Damaging winds, large hail and tornadoes were reported with the storms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The tornado touched down along the eastern shore of the Illinois River in the Hadley Landing Public Access Area.  Two cabins sustained minor roof and siding damage while a third cabin sustained severe damage.  A fourth cabin was totally destroyed.  Numerous large trees were also severed or snapped off near the base of the trunk just east of the cabin.  Debris from the destroyed cabin extended over 300 yards to the east northeast.  The damage over this area was rated low end EF2.  The tornado travelled northeastward and caused additional tree damage in the flood plain.  The tornado approached the intersection of Illinois Route 100 and Coon Creek Road.  A farmstead at this location sustained varying degrees of damage.  One machine shed sustained roof and side damage while a small garage was totally destroyed.  The farm home sustained minor roof damage before the tornado lifted and dissipated.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tornado touched down just southwest of Prairie.  As it travelled to the east northeast it damaged 3 homes on Richfield Road. Two of the homes sustained minor roof damage and the other had a portion of its roof removed. The tornado lifted and dissipated just northeast of Prairie.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A deepening low pressure center tracked north-northeast from northeast Texas to eastern Missouri. A strong southerly wind flow brought abundant moisture northward across the Lower Ohio Valley and southeast Missouri. Widespread rainfall amounts from two to four inches occurred.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A woman drove into a flooded area along a rural road northeast of Pinckneyville. She was rescued safely from the hood of her vehicle by sheriff and emergency medical personnel. She was treated for hypothermia at a local hospital.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front moved through the region triggering showers and thunderstorms. Damaging winds, large hail and tornadoes were reported with the storms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tornado touched down 2 miles east northeast of Baldwin and travelled to the northeast.  It removed the roof from a cinder block outbuilding and tossed it across Doza Creek Road.  Also, several trees and branches were snapped along its path.  It lifted and dissipated 4 miles west southwest of Tilden.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front moved through the region triggering showers and thunderstorms. Damaging winds, large hail and tornadoes were reported with the storms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tornado touched down 2.5 miles west southwest of the entrance to the Washington County Conservation Area on Illinois Route 127 and travelled to the northeast.  It snapped off numerous trees and branches before lifting and dissipating four tenths of a mile north of the conservation area entrance.

Eighteen inches of water was reported in the intersection of Mooney Drive and Armour Road.  Storm total rainfall in Bourbonnais was measured at 3.47 inches.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The U.S. Highway 51 tunnel under railroad tracks on the north side of Cairo was flooded.

Temperatures during the early morning hours ranged from zero to 2 below zero with wind chills 10 below to 15 below zero.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A frontal boundary wavered across the area between the 6th and 11th, bringing scattered showers and storms each day. Total rainfall was generally 1 to 1.5 inches. Because the ground was still so wet from heavy precipitation in late January, there was some minor flooding along rivers.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Minor flooding occurred on the Wabash River. At the Mount Carmel gage, the river crested at 24.86 feet on the 19th. Flood stage there is 19 feet. Extensive lowland flooding threatened some mobile homes. Installation of flood gate A at Mount Carmel was necessary. Access to local camp sites was blocked, but there was minimal impact to commercial activities because of gate closure. Several local roads were impassable. Water flowed over River Road just north of Fifth Street in the Mount Carmel area. All oil field production ceased with the exception of pumping units on substructures. Access to these was by boat only. Farmers moved livestock to higher ground.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A frontal boundary wavered across the area between the 6th and 11th, bringing scattered showers and storms each day. Total rainfall was generally 1 to 1.5 inches. Because the ground was still so wet from heavy precipitation in late January, there was some minor flooding along rivers.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Minor flooding of the Wabash River occurred. At the New Harmony, IN gage, the river crested at 17.56 feet on the 21st. Flood stage there is 15 feet. Low-lying bottomlands along the river were flooded.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong to severe thunderstorms moved across northern Illinois during the morning and afternoon hours of March 8th.  Heavy rain and flooding were also reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Nickel size hail was reported near mile marker 308 on Interstate 57.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong to severe thunderstorms moved across northern Illinois during the morning and afternoon hours of March 8th.  Heavy rain and flooding were also reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An EF0 tornado touched down on county road 1500N between 1100E and 1200E.  A detached garage was destroyed at this location and the debris was deposited in a field to the northeast.  A hay barn at this same location suffered little damage although a trailer camper parked inside the barn was pushed through the wooden front door by straight line winds which were funneled through the open back of the barn.

Another garage and another steel building were completely destroyed.  Debris from this location was lifted and deposited in a field and treeline approximately 400 yards to the northeast.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Two to three inches of rain in a 24 to 36-hour period caused many area creeks and streams to overflow their banks.  One man had to be rescued from his vehicle when he attempted to cross a road covered by water from a nearby creek.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A man was rescued from his vehicle near LaFayette in northeastern Knox County when he tried to cross a roadway flooded by a nearby creek.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Two to three inches of rain falling on already saturated soils caused many area creeks and streams to overflow their banks.  As a result, several county roads were flooded along and north of a Rushville to Minonk line.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms moved across parts of north central Illinois during the afternoon of May 7th producing strong winds.

An area of low pressure tracked form the lower Mississippi River Valley into the eastern Great Lakes on March 29th, bringing a swath of snow across west-central Illinois.  In general, locations west of I-57 picked up between 2 and 4 inches of snow...with isolated amounts of up to 6 inches where thunder was reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Cooperative Observer in St. David recorded 6.7 inches of snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong to severe thunderstorms moved across northern Illinois during the evening hours of May 13th into the early morning hours of May 14th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Dozens of trees were uprooted along 4000N, northwest of Momence.  The fallen trees damaged vehicles and flattened a large pole barn.  Just down the road, a roof of an outbuilding was partially removed.  At the same location the roof of a house was torn off.  Pieces of twisted metal from the roofs of barns and sheds were found in trees and wrapped around nearby utility poles.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong to severe thunderstorms moved across northern Illinois during the evening hours of May 13th into the early morning hours of May 14th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The roof of a grain bin was blown off along Lowell Park Road.  Debris from the bin was strewn several hundred feet away into a nearby field.  A large pine tree near the bin was twisted off and a window of a house was broke.  Part of a roof of an apartment building was blown off in downtown Mount Morris.

A storm system tracked from Nebraska eastward across central Illinois on January 14th, bringing total snowfall accumulations of 3 to 5 inches.  As the system moved further east, strong northwesterly winds developed across the area, creating considerable blowing and drifting snow.  Numerous traffic accidents occurred as the snow was initially falling, particularly during the morning hours of January 14th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A woman lost control of her car on Illinois Route 121 near Mt. Pulaski at around 8 AM on January 14th.  Her vehicle slid into the path of an oncoming semi-truck and crashed.  The driver of the car was pronounced dead at the scene.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A fast-moving storm system tracked from the Plains states eastward across central Illinois on January 25th, producing a snow accumulation of between 1 and 2 inches.  Several traffic accidents were reported across the area due to snow-covered roads.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A six-car accident occurred on I-55 in Sprigfield at around 10:30 AM on January 25th.  Snow-covered roads were cited as the cause of the accident.  Two people were taken to a local hospital with minor injuries.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A fast-moving storm system tracked from the Plains states eastward across central Illinois on January 25th, producing a snow accumulation of between 1 and 2 inches.  Several traffic accidents were reported across the area due to snow-covered roads.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A woman lost control of her car on U.S. 45 about 1 mile south of 650th Avenue at 5:55 PM.  Snow-covered roads were to blame for the accident.  She was taken to an area hospital with minor injuries.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong dome of high pressure aloft brought very hot and humid conditions. On the afternoon of the 26th, the heat index peaked around 105 degrees in the far southwest corner of Illinois, from Carbondale south and southeast. On the 27th, the heat index peaked around 105 in the southernmost counties near Cairo. Actual high temperatures were in the mid to upper 90's both days.

Thunderstorms rapidly developed along a surface frontal boundary from central Illinois into southern Missouri. Extreme instability and favorable wind shear ahead of the activity promoted organized and isolated supercell storms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A small shed was overturned. Several trees and limbs were downed. Peak winds were estimated near 70 mph in this small tornado.

Heavy rains from May 13th to May 15th resulted in the La Moine River near Colmar going above the moderate flood stage level of 22 feet around 9 pm on May 15 and the major flood stage level of 24 feet around 1:30 am on May 17.  It crested around 24.84 feet around 6 pm on May 17.  It fell back below the major flood stage level around 10:30 am on May 18 and the moderate flood stage level around 6 am on May 19.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rains from May 13th to May 15th resulted in the La Moine River near Colmar going above the moderate flood stage level of 22 feet around 9 pm on May 15 and the major flood stage level of 24 feet around 1:30 am on May 17. It crested around 24.84 feet around 6 pm on May 17. It fell back below the major flood stage level around 10:30 am on May 18 and the moderate flood stage level around 6 am on May 19.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms developed ahead of upper level impulses over far southeast Iowa and southwest Missouri. The atmosphere became quite unstable, with mixed layer CAPE values approaching 3000 late in the afternoon. This instability, along with 20 to 30 knots of deep layer wind shear, favored a few multicell severe storms with damaging winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree and a power pole blew down at Cisne. Just north and northwest of Geff, shingles were blown off and fences were down.

Heavy rains during the last four days of April resulted in the La Moine River near Colmar going above the moderate flood stage level of 22 feet on May 1 around 9:30 am. It crested around 22.8 feet around 2 am May 2. It fell back below the moderate flood stage level around 1 am May 3.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An isolated severe thunderstorm developed along a stationary frontal zone in southeast Illinois.  Deep layer wind shear was marginally favorable for organized storm structures.

A front wavered across the region from the 8th to the 17th, producing daily rounds of showers and thunderstorms. Many of these storms produced rainfall rates of 1 to 3 inches per hour. The Mississippi River rose above flood stage for a few days.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Minor flooding of the Mississippi River occurred, affecting mostly bottomland fields. On the river gage at Thebes, the river crested at 33.64 feet on the 22nd. This was less than a foot above the flood stage of 33 feet.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An organized complex of thunderstorms developed ahead of a cold front as it approached southern Illinois and southeast Missouri during the hottest and most unstable time of day. Very strong instability, coupled with moderately strong mid-level winds, resulted in numerous damaging wind events. Most of the damaging winds were associated with small-scale bows that raced southeast ahead of the main storm complex.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Two pilots reported a funnel cloud.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A downburst moved through portions of Alton, blowing down the third story of an old brick building on East Broadway. Some of the bricks and debris were thrown onto the roofs of adjacent buildings.  Further down the block the winds blew in a wall of an abandoned lumber company's metal storage building.  Numerous vehicles in the area sustained damage from the flying debris as well.  Several large trees in the area were blown over as well.  One tree fell onto the roof of a house causing minor damage.  No one was injured due to this event.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The damage west of and in the city limits of Gillespie, Illinois was caused by downburst winds and one small tornado.  The survey of the damage indicated an intermittent tornado track from 1.5 miles west of Gillespie to the west side of Gillespie at the High School.  Several homes and outbuidings were damaged along with a power pole.  The tornado was 30 to 40 yards wide and traveled adjacent to Illinois Route 16 towards Gillespie.  A building on the high school campus lost its roof and the damage was rated EF0 on the Enhanced Fujita Scale. Then the tornado lifted and dissipated.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Directly south of the tornado track downburst winds caused widespread damage, that continued through the center of Gillespie.  Numerous buildings had roof damage and many trees were blown over or snapped off.  Wind speed was estimated to have been between 60 and 80 mph within the downburst swath.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front moved through the region, triggering showers and thunderstorms. Numerous reports of large hail, a tornado and some wind damage occurred with these storms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The tornado touched down just northeast of the intersection of North Green Mount Road and Lebanon Avenue in the Eagles Landing Subdivision, where up to 6 homes sustained minor to moderate damage.  From this point the tornado produced a nearly continuous damage path that extended to about 1 mile southeast of the intersection of Highway 4 and Jefferson Road.  It finally lifted as it approached the Kaskaskia River.  The total path length of the tornado is estimated to be 12.8 miles, with an average width of damage of about one-tenth of a mile.  However, straight line winds did cause lesser damage over a wider swath of up to 3 miles. The greatest damage was to a home located just west of the intersection of Highway 4 and Jefferson Road.  Damage there was rated EF2 on the Enhanced Fujita Scale.  Elsewhere along the path of the tornado, the damage was generally in the EF0-EF1 range.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front moved through the region, triggering showers and thunderstorms. Numerous reports of large hail, a tornado and some wind damage occurred with these storms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm winds caused damage in O'Fallon and Shiloh.  The junior high school in O'Fallon sustained roof damage.  A two foot diameter tree was blown down onto an unoccupied truck on U.S. Highway 50 near the intersection with N. Green Mount Road.  Numerous other trees and  power lines were blown down.  One tree landed on a house causing moderate damage near the intersection of Main Street and Caroline Street in O'Fallon.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm winds caused minor damage in the Brownstown area.  Three miles west of Brownstown a few windows were blown out of a trailer. Just south of Brownstown several large tree limbs were blown down.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front moved through the region, triggering showers and thunderstorms. Numerous reports of large hail, a tornado and some wind damage occurred with these storms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Up to three inches of rain fell in a short amount of time causing flash flooding.  Numerous roads had water over them including Illinois Route 4 at School View Drive and on Lickenbrock School Road a mile north of Darmstadt Road.

A strong low level jet stream and abundant moisture for late winter produced scattered, elevated thunderstorms in central Illinois early in the morning. One of these thunderstorms produced a lightning strike which did extensive damage to a home near Decatur.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Lightning struck a house on the west side of Forsyth causing a fire, and extensive damage to the home. There were no injuries, although the family dog had to be rescued by fire personnel.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weak area of low pressure moving through the Ohio Valley produced 1 to 3 inches of snow in north central and eastern Illinois. Somewhat higher amounts of 4 to 5 inches were reported in Knox County. The snowfall produced slippery travel conditions and numerous auto accidents.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: One to two inches of snow resulted in three separate traffic accidents on I-72 near Monticello between 8:45 AM and 9:00 AM. The first accident resulted in an indirect fatality and three injuries, while two more injuries were a result of the next two accidents in the same vicinity.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: As low pressure exited the area, strong westerly winds swept across central Illinois during the afternoon and evening of February 11th.  Wind gusts as high as 50 to 60 mph were reported, resulting in minor damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: High winds knocked a light pole onto a vehicle at Main and Bowman Streets in Danville.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: As low pressure exited the area, strong westerly winds swept across central Illinois during the afternoon and evening of February 11th.  Wind gusts as high as 50 to 60 mph were reported, resulting in minor damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: High winds blew a road construction sign onto a vehicle in Springfield.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A line of thunderstorms crossed southern Illinois, then increased in intensity as it moved east across western Kentucky into an increasingly unstable atmosphere. Damaging surface winds were enhanced by a strong 50-knot rear-inflow jet feeding into the complex. Most of the damaging winds occurred south of Illinois.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several trees were blown down.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: As low pressure exited the area, strong westerly winds swept across central Illinois during the afternoon and evening of February 11th.  Wind gusts as high as 50 to 60 mph were reported, resulting in minor damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: High winds knocked a tree down onto IL Route 1, approximately one mile south of Georgetown.  The downed tree then resulted in a vehicle accident.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A persistent upper level low in west central Illinois resulted in slow moving, training thunderstorms during the afternoon and evening. Rainfall amounts ranged from 2.00 to 4.00 inches...with isolated pockets of 5.00 inch totals in Sangamon and Macon counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Slow moving thunderstorms produced rainfall rates of 2.00 inches per hour and flash flooding in western and central Sangamon County, including the western suburbs of Springfield. The heaviest rain was from New Berlin to the west side of Springfield where 2.50 to 3.50 inches was reported. Numerous streets and rural roads were flooded.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low pressure center tracked into central Illinois by early afternoon, producing a narrow band of intense rain in parts of west central Illinois and a small part of southeast Illinois. Isolated rainfall totals of 4.00 to 5.00 inches resulted in flash flooding. A man drowned in Morgan County as a result of the flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Rainfall amounts of 4.50 to 5.00 inches were reported within two hours in a 15 mile wide band across western and central Morgan County. Numerous streets in the city of Jacksonville were flooded. Near the town of Woodson, a man drowned while he was trying to clear debris from an intake valve and culvert near a flooded pond.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Heavy rains during the middle of the month resulted in the Rock River at Joslin going above the moderate flood stage level of 14 feet on June 22nd around 12 am. It crested around 14.73 feet around 830 pm on June 23. It fell below the moderate flood stage level around 5 pm June 24.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rains during the middle of the month resulted in the Rock River at Joslin going above the moderate flood stage level of 14 feet on June 22nd around 12 am. It crested around 14.73 feet around 830 pm on June 23. It fell below the moderate flood stage level around 5 pm June 24.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Two slow-moving cold fronts affected southern Illinois. The first front on the 11th and 12th caused torrential downpours and training storms. The other slow-moving front brought scattered thunderstorms from the 15th to the 17th. The heavy rainfall sent the Big Muddy River above flood stage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Minor flooding occurred on the Big Muddy River. At the Murphysboro gage, the river crested at 24.63 feet on the 17th. Flood stage is 22 feet. Some low-lying fields and parklands in the flood plain were flooded.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Moderate to strong instability developed near a stalled front across the Lower Ohio Valley. Despite seasonably weak winds aloft, isolated occurrences of wind damage and heavy rain were reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A spotter reported 0.74 inches of rainfall were received in ten minutes. Water was ponding on Route 37.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A large and slow-moving complex of thunderstorms developed in a southerly flow of moist and unstable air ahead of a cold front over central Missouri and central Illinois. The storms produced torrential rain.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Highway flooding was reported along Highway 148 near Waltonville and along Highway 15 near Woodlawn.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A large and slow-moving complex of thunderstorms developed in a southerly flow of moist and unstable air ahead of a cold front over central Missouri and central Illinois. The storms produced torrential rain.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Total rainfall of 2.41 inches was reported by a co-operative observer.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A warm front returned north across the region, causing thunderstorms with torrential downpours.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Standing water was on many streets in the community. Ditches were overflowing. A spotter measured 2.72 inches of rain in just one hour.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms with torrential rain trained along a stationary front over the Lower Ohio Valley. A shortwave trough tracked eastward from Arkansas and phased with a second shortwave that tracked east from the upper Mississippi River Valley. These shortwaves provided uplift for sustained intense convection.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Water was over two main roads, including Highway 142.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Multicell clusters of thunderstorms intensified while spreading east-southeast in advance of a remnant upper level disturbance. A belt of 30 to 40 knot mid-level winds and strong instability supported the storms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A new barn that was under construction in Anna City Park was blown down. The new barn was to replace a structure which sustained major damage in the January winter storm.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Multicell clusters of thunderstorms intensified while spreading east-southeast in advance of a remnant upper level disturbance. A belt of 30 to 40 knot mid-level winds and strong instability supported the storms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Lots of standing water was observed in town. Water was flowing over the intersection of old U.S. Route 51 and a side street. Flooding of this intersection was seen as a rare occurrence by longtime residents of the community. A co-operative observer measured 3.28 inches of rain over a two-day period, ending with the storms.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Lightning struck a tall white oak tree and may have started a fire at a telephone company switching building.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weak area of high pressure, a clear sky and a saturated ground from recent rainfall resulted in widespread dense fog across all of central and southeast Illinois. In Logan County, a man died in a traffic accident attributed to the dense fog.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Dense fog with zero visibility at times contributed to a traffic accident which killed a man in Logan County. The man was traveling on I-155, about 8 miles north of Lincoln, when he lost control of his vehicle near a curve, causing the vehicle to roll over around 11:39 PM CST.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front moved through the region triggering numerous showers and thunderstorms.  Some of the storms produced severe weather including large hail and damaging winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tornado touched down about 2.5 miles south southeast of Clayton and travelled to the east.  It only caused damage to trees and tree limbs in the area before crossing into Brown county.

Scattered thunderstorms developed ahead of a fast moving cold front, and moved into central Illinois during the evening hours. One of these thunderstorms produced large hail in a small part of Tazewell County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Hail up to the size of pennies was reported near Mackinaw.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A warm front lifting through central Illinois during the late evening of June 7th produced scattered thunderstorms. One of these thunderstorms produced a lightning bolt which struck a mobile home near Henry and destroyed it.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Lightning struck a mobile home near Henry and started a fire. The fire destroyed the mobile home. An occupant of the structure sustained minor burns from the fire.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A nearly stationary frontal boundary triggered marginally severe thunderstorms across central and southeast Illinois during the afternoon and early evening of June 2nd.|Microbursts caused localized wind damage in Sangamon and Lawrence counties.

A nearly stationary frontal boundary triggered marginally severe thunderstorms across central and southeast Illinois during the afternoon and early evening of June 2nd.|Microbursts caused localized wind damage in Sangamon and Lawrence counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The roof of a pole barn was lifted off by strong winds.  The building had no doors and was open to the south.  Based on radar data, a microburst with winds of between 50 and 60 mph likely caused the damage.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front moving eastward across central Illinois triggered scattered thunderstorms during the late afternoon and evening hours of June 8th.  A few reports of hail were received from Douglas County.

A weak cold front dropped into north-central Illinois during the late afternoon and early evening hours of June 25th.  An isolated thunderstorm developed and became severe across McLean County, producing golf-ball sized hail in Towanda.

A strong cold front approaching the Mississippi River triggered a line of strong to severe thunderstorms across west-central Illinois during the evening of June 27th.  Numerous reports of wind damage were received, primarily along and west of the I-55 corridor.  A fatality occurred in McLean County when a canopy blown into the road by a non-severe gust front caused a head-on collision.

Strong winds developed over northern Illinois during the evening of October 6th and continued into the early morning hours of October 7th.  Wind gusts were generally between 40 mph and 50 mph.  A peak wind gust of 53 mph was measured at Midway Airport and a peak wind gust of 47 mph was measured at O'Hare Airport.  Numerous tree limbs and power lines were blown down.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Six large tree limbs were blown down in Morton Grove which brought down power lines.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A winter storm moved across northern Illinois during the afternoon and evening hours of December 23rd.  A mixture of sleet, snow and freezing rain changed to all freezing rain by evening with many locations receiving around a quarter inch of ice accumulation on trees, power lines and other elevated surfaces.  Some areas in north central Illinois received as much as one half inch of ice acumulation.  Numerous trees, tree limbs and power lines fell down due to the weight of the ice.  While some ice accumulation was reported on less traveled and untreated roads, most roadways remained wet even with temperatures near or slightly below freezing.  The precipitation diminished during the morning hours of December 24th.

Heavy snow began falling during the early morning hours of December 8th and continued into the afternoon hours of December 9th.  Strong winds caused blowing and drifting snow during the evening of December 9th and morning of December 10th.  Stormtotal snowfall amounts included 9.5 inches 3 miles north northwest of Dixon; 8.6 inches 2 miles west southwest of Hebron; 8.2 inches in Rockford; 8.1 inches 1.6 miles east southeast of Roscoe; 7.8 inches 2.5 miles west northwest of Bull Valley; 7.6 inches near Ashton; 7.0 inches 3.2 miles southwest of Popular Grove; 6.7 inches 0.8 miles west northwest of Wonder Lake and 6.6 inches 3.1 miles east northeast of Machesney Park.

Temperatures climbed to near 90 on June 22nd and into the middle 90s on June 23rd, 24th and 25th with afternoon heat indices in the upper 90s.  Overnight low temperatures ranged from the lower to middle 70s.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An 11 year old girl suffered a heat stroke after being left outside in very hot conditions on June 25th in the 9300 block of South Green Street in Chicago.  A 59 year old woman died from heat stress in her home early on June 26th on Rice Street in Chicago.  A 39 year old man died from heat stress in June 23rd on 95th Street in Hickory Hills.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A 51 year old man died on Ashland Avenue in Chicago due to cold exposure with temperatures in the lower to middle 20s.
Strong to severe thunderstorms developed over parts of northeast Illinois during the late afternoon and evening hours of July 16th.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wind gusts were estimated to 60 mph with 4 to 5 inch diameter tree limbs blown down.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Lightning struck a new auto body repair building on Grinnell Road and started a fire.  The building along with 3 vehicles was completely destroyed.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level system moved northeast through the region, triggering showers and thunderstorms.  Some of the storms produced large hail, damaging winds and tornadoes.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: National Weather Service personnel did  a survey of the damage in the Vandalia area and determined that it was straight line wind damage.  Numerous large tree limbs, trees and power lines were blown down.  Overall the damage was produced by 50 to 60 mph winds.  However, there were two areas of damage that suggested slightly higher wind speeds.  One area was a corn field about a mile west of the U.S. Highway 40-Interstate 70 interchange that was completely flattened.  The other area of slightly higher winds was over southern sections of Vandalia.  Tree damage here was a bit more extensive and two large trees were totally uprooted by the winds.  There was also some minor damage to two metal roofs in the area.

The storms then raced eastward across central and southeast Illinois, producing wind gusts of between 60 and 70 mph.  Numerous trees and power lines were blown down in a wide swath from the Mississippi River eastward to the Indiana border.

Also, a retirement community building had several windows blown out and numerous trees, tree limbs and power lines were blown down.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A persistent upper level low in west central Illinois resulted in slow moving, training thunderstorms during the afternoon and evening. Rainfall amounts ranged from 2.00 to 4.00 inches...with isolated pockets of 5.00 inch totals in Sangamon and Macon counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Slow moving thunderstorms produced rainfall rates of 2.00 inches per hour and flash flooding in northwest Macon County. The heaviest rain was near Warrensburg where 4.00 to 5.00 inches was reported. Numerous streets and rural roads were flooded.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe thunderstorms developed during the afternoon hours and moved northeast across parts northern and central Illinois.  Three tornadoes were confirmed during this event.

Strong to severe thunderstorms developed over parts of northeast Illinois during the late afternoon and evening hours of July 16th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Small tree limbs were blown down near Butterfield Road and Park Boulevard.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Isolated thunderstorms developed in parts of central and eastern Illinois ahead of a strong low pressure area in the upper Midwest, and its associated cold front. One of these thunderstorms produced lightning which caused a fire in Champaign County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A six unit apartment building on the west side of Champaign was struck by lightning, which caused a fire. Walls in one of the apartments were damaged, as well as a furnace. All of the occupants of the building escaped unharmed.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An intensifying, slow moving area of low pressure moved into central Illinois from late on October 22 into October 23. The result was a prolonged period of heavy rain on already saturated ground. Rainfall amounts in a 24 to 30 hour period ranged from 2.00 to 3.50 inches of rain. The heavy rain and saturated ground caused significant damage to a road in DeWitt County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rainfall around 3.00 inches in a 24 hour period, on already saturated ground, caused a portion of Sumpter Road, just south of its intersection with Carle Springs Road, to wash out. Standing water over the road hid the damage to the road. A truck trying to cross the water covered road fell into the hole created by washed out surface. The driver was not injured.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A line of severe thunderstorms moved slowly southeast through the early morning hours. These storms moved through a very moist and unstable atmosphere. Small-scale bow echoes produced locally damaging winds and isolated large hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A roadway was flooded.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Several rounds of heavy rain over much of the Mississippi River Valley caused many rivers to rise above flood stage. May continued the active pattern begun in April. Several of these events produced very heavy rainfall. Rainfall was one to two inches above normal in most spots for the month.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Minor to moderate flooding of the Mississippi River occurred for nearly the entire month of May. At the Thebes gage in Alexander County, where flood stage is 33 feet, the river crested at 38.35 feet on the 23rd. Moderate flooding begins around 37 feet there. Agricultural bottomland and low-lying areas were flooded.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Several rounds of heavy rain over much of the Mississippi River Valley caused many rivers to rise above flood stage. May continued the active pattern begun in April. Several of these events produced very heavy rainfall. Rainfall was one to two inches above normal in most spots for the month.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Minor flooding of the Big Muddy River occurred. At the Plumfield gage, where flood stage is 20 feet, the river crested at 23.30 feet on the 11th. Low-lying and bottomland areas were flooded.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A very intense storm system tracked from the Oklahoma Panhandle to western Illinois and then to Lake Huron. This had the lowest barometric pressure (975 mb or 28.79 inches) in the Midwest since the November 10, 1998 storm that also moved through the upper Midwest. This powerful winter storm produced very heavy snowfall, blizzard conditions and bitterly cold temperatures on December 8-9, 2009. Snow amounts of at least 10 to 15 inches were common north of a Fairfield, Iowa to Sterling, Illinois line. The largest amount was an incredible 17.7 inches at Orangeville, Illinois in Stephenson county. Other amounts included 15 inches at Brighton, Iowa (Washington county), Mt. Vernon, Iowa (Linn county) and at Freeport, Illinois (Stephenson county). Snowfall was much lighter in southeast Iowa, extreme northeast Missouri and west central Illinois. Here snow accumulations were 1 to 4 inches as rain and sleet mixed with the snow. A band of freezing rain from Burlington, Iowa to Sterling, Illinois coated trees and power lines with up to 3 tenths of an inch of ice. A few locations also reported thundersnows with snowfall rates of nearly 2 inches per hour. There were scattered power outages and some tree branches down. Numerous accidents and vehicles sliding into ditches were reported across the region. Many schools and businesses were also closed. In addition, winds gusting over 50 mph produced widespread blizzard conditions across much of eastern Iowa and extreme northeast Missouri, reducing visibilities to near zero on December 9.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A trained spotter at Chadwick reported 10.8 inches of snow.

An upper air disturbance, combined with a warm front lifting across the region, produced a band of light snow over northeast Iowa and northwest Illinois on December 21-22, 2009. Snow amounts were mainly in the 2 to 5 inch range with the highest total of 5.4 inches measured near Mt. Carroll, Illinois in Carroll county.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A trained spotter 5 miles northeast of Mt. Carroll measured 5.4 inches of snow.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A COOP observer at Stockton reported 3.6 inches of snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A slow moving upper level low pressure system over the region produced a prolonged period of light to moderate snow during the weekend of December 25-27, 2009. Over the 3-day period widespread snow accumulations of 3 to 8 inches were noted, with the most measured at Good Hope, Illinois in McDonough county with 9 inches.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A CoCoRaHS observer at Davis measured 5.1 inches of snow during this 3-day event.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An ice storm occurred on December 23, 2009 in portions of eastern Iowa and northwest Illinois. Ice accumulations of 1/4 to 1/2 inch were common which coated trees and power lines but temperatures in the lower 30s kept most roads wet. Scattered power outages and some broken tree branches were reported as winds gusted to 35 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A very intense storm system tracked from the Oklahoma Panhandle to western Illinois and then to Lake Huron. This had the lowest barometric pressure (975 mb or 28.79 inches) in the Midwest since the November 10, 1998 storm that also moved through the upper Midwest. This powerful winter storm produced very heavy snowfall, blizzard conditions and bitterly cold temperatures on December 8-9, 2009. Snow amounts of at least 10 to 15 inches were common north of a Fairfield, Iowa to Sterling, Illinois line. The largest amount was an incredible 17.7 inches at Orangeville, Illinois in Stephenson county. Other amounts included 15 inches at Brighton, Iowa (Washington county), Mt. Vernon, Iowa (Linn county) and at Freeport, Illinois (Stephenson county). Snowfall was much lighter in southeast Iowa, extreme northeast Missouri and west central Illinois. Here snow accumulations were 1 to 4 inches as rain and sleet mixed with the snow. A band of freezing rain from Burlington, Iowa to Sterling, Illinois coated trees and power lines with up to 3 tenths of an inch of ice. A few locations also reported thundersnows with snowfall rates of nearly 2 inches per hour. There were scattered power outages and some tree branches down. Numerous accidents and vehicles sliding into ditches were reported across the region. Many schools and businesses were also closed. In addition, winds gusting over 50 mph produced widespread blizzard conditions across much of eastern Iowa and extreme northeast Missouri, reducing visibilities to near zero on December 9.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper air disturbance, combined with a warm front lifting across the region, produced a band of light snow over northeast Iowa and northwest Illinois on December 21-22, 2009. Snow amounts were mainly in the 2 to 5 inch range with the highest total of 5.4 inches measured near Mt. Carroll, Illinois in Carroll county.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A trained spotter at Ridott measured 4.8 inches of snow, while Freeport picked up 4.0 inches of snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: As a deep 500 mb trough developed over the Plains, warmer and more moist air streamed across the Mississippi Valley. This fetch of moisture produced a large area of showers. Storm total rainfall amounts were between 3 and 5 inches over southeast Missouri and southern Illinois from the 23rd to the 25th. As a result, a number of rivers rose above flood stage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Minor flooding of the Big Muddy River occurred. At the Murphysboro gage, the river crested at 27.53 feet on the 30th. Flood stage there is 22 feet. Minor flooding of low-lying fields and pastureland occurred.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered thunderstorms persisted through the early morning hours along an axis of increasing moisture and confluence associated with a west-southwesterly low level jet. The activity was located on the southern fringe of a moderately strong east-west mid-level jet. The associated deep-layer shear and warm advection boosted the threat of large hail and isolated wind damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Golf-ball size hail fell across much of western Williamson County from Carterville to Herrin and Cambria.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Several rounds of heavy rain over much of the Ohio River Valley caused many rivers to rise above flood stage. May continued the active pattern begun in April. Several of these events produced very heavy rainfall. Rainfall was one to two inches above normal in most spots for the month.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Moderate flooding of the Ohio River occurred. At the Grand Chain gage, where flood stage is 42 feet, the river crested at 51.60 feet on the 12th. Agricultural bottomland and low-lying areas were flooded. Water reached the power house at Lock and Dam 53.

A line of severe thunderstorms moved slowly southeast through the early morning hours. These storms moved through a very moist and unstable atmosphere. Small-scale bow echoes produced locally damaging winds and isolated large hail.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A winter storm moved across northern Illinois during the afternoon and evening hours of December 23rd.  A mixture of sleet, snow and freezing rain changed to all freezing rain by evening with many locations receiving around a quarter inch of ice accumulation on trees, power lines and other elevated surfaces.  Some areas in north central Illinois received as much as one half inch of ice acumulation.  Numerous trees, tree limbs and power lines fell down due to the weight of the ice.  While some ice accumulation was reported on less traveled and untreated roads, most roadways remained wet even with temperatures near or slightly below freezing.  The precipitation diminished during the morning hours of December 24th.

A strong area of low pressure moving into the Midwest late on 10/29 and early on 10/30 tapped plenty of tropical moisture. It produced a prolonged period of heavy rainfall which resulted in widespread areal flooding in central Illinois between October 30th and November 3rd. Rain amounts of 2.00 to 3.00 inches were reported in much of central Illinois during a 30 hour period from late on 10/29 through 10/30/09. This was in addition to the more than 3.00 inches of rain which fell the previous week. Many rural roads, creeks and streams were flooded. A man in Logan County drowned when he attempted to cross a water covered roadway.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Numeruous creeks, streams, ditches and rural roads in Logan County began flooding during the morning hours of October 30th. A 97 year old man drowned when he attempted to cross a water covered road about 0.5 mile south of Mt. Pulaski around 1030 AM CST October 31st. The flooding finally subsided late in the morning on November 3rd.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: As a deep 500 mb trough developed over the Plains, warmer and more moist air streamed across the Mississippi Valley. This fetch of moisture produced a large area of showers. Storm total rainfall amounts were between 3 and 5 inches over southeast Missouri and southern Illinois from the 23rd to the 25th. As a result, a number of rivers rose above flood stage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Minor flooding of the Ohio River occurred. At the Cairo river gage, the river rose above flood stage on the 30th and crested at 40.35 feet on the 31st. Flood stage there is 40 feet. Only very minor flooding of bottomland fields and parkland occurred.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A deepening low pressure system lifted northeast across northern Illinois into Lower Michigan. On the back side of the low, strong west winds gusted to around 45 mph. The peak wind gust in southern Illinois was 50 mph, measured atop a 50-foot tower near Round Knob in Massac County. Some tree limbs were blown down, but no damage or major problems were reported. Scattered power outages occurred. The city of DuQuoin in Perry County was affected by power outages caused by high winds tossing power lines into each other. Limbs were down in the DuQuoin area of Perry County and parts of Union County.

A deepening low pressure center tracked north-northeast from northeast Texas to eastern Missouri, then eventually into Iowa. Strong southeast winds gradually turned into the southwest. Peak gusts were generally from 40 to 45 mph across southern Illinois. A rooftop anemometer at a house near Round Knob in Massac County measured a gust to 52 mph. A 30-foot tower at the same location measured a peak gust of 41 mph.

Scattered power outages occurred. The city of DuQuoin in Perry County was affected by power outages caused by high winds tossing power lines into each other. Limbs were down in the DuQuoin area of Perry County and parts of Union County.

A deepening low pressure center tracked north-northeast from northeast Texas to eastern Missouri, then eventually into Iowa. Strong southeast winds gradually turned into the southwest. Peak gusts were generally from 40 to 45 mph across southern Illinois. A rooftop anemometer at a house near Round Knob in Massac County measured a gust to 52 mph. A 30-foot tower at the same location measured a peak gust of 41 mph.

With cold air in place and sufficient moisture content in the lowest levels of the atmosphere, freezing fog developed along the Ohio River in southernmost Illinois. Several accidents were reported on the Interstate 24 bridge crossing the Ohio River. The Ohio River bridge on U.S. Highway 60/51 near Cairo was closed for about an hour due to accidents. Icy conditions were reported on many other bridges and overpasses. Visibility was reduced to as low as one mile at the airports near Metropolis and Cairo.

Visibility in those areas was one-quarter mile or less. A light accretion of ice was observed, mostly on elevated surfaces such as vehicles.

A winter storm moved northeast across the Ohio Valley on January 9th and 10th.  Snow developed over northern Illinois during the mid to late evening of January 9th and continued into the late afternoon of January 10th.  Storm total snowfall amounts included 12.0 inches in Evanston, 11.1 inches in Beach Park, 11.0 inches in Des Plaines, 10.9 inches in Lake Bluff, 10.7 inches in Arlington Heights, 10.2 inches at O'Hare Airport, 9.9 inches in Buffalo Grove, 9.6 inches in Northbrook, 9.2 inches in Streamwood, 9.1 inches in Mundelein, 9.0 inches in Rockton, 8.7 inches in Roscoe, 8.7 inches in Fox Lake, 8.6 inches in Carol Stream and 8.2 inches in Wauconda.

Very cold temperatures combined with strong winds to create dangerously low wind chill values on January 15th which continued into the morning hours of January 16th.  Low temperatures on the morning of January 15th dropped to 15 below to 20 below zero with a few colder locations.  Wind chill readings for most of January 15th ranged from 30 below to 45 below zero.

Light accumulations of ice formed on elevated surfaces, including vehicles and highway overpasses. Up to one-eighth inch of ice was on vehicle windshields. A couple of semis wrecked on Interstate 24 westbound in northern Johnson County. One of the semis overturned, seriously injuring the driver and spilling the truck's cargo across the highway. The interstate was closed for several hours. At the Kentucky state line, the Interstate 24 bridge over the Ohio River was closed for a short time due to several accidents on the bridge. A few cars were off the road on Interstate 57 near West Frankfort in Franklin County. Multiple accidents occurred in Gallatin County, and the Shawneetown bridge over the Ohio River was impassable. Scattered slick spots were reported on roads throughout the region. A low pressure system skirted just southeast of the region, bringing a wintry mix of precipitation.

Low temperatures dropped into the middle to lower teens on January 4th and 5th.  A 56 year old man in Chicago died due to the cold.  In Davis Junction, a 47 year old man apparently abondoned his car and began walking.  His car was found along the side of a road on January 4th.  His body was discovered in a ditch on January 20th about 700 feet from where his car was found.  His death was determined to be from hypothermia.

A prolonged, major winter storm dumped 6 to 10 inches of sleet and snow north of a line from East Cape Girardeau through Vienna to the confluence of the Wabash and Ohio Rivers. This resulted in at least two dozen roof collapses and very difficult driving conditions. South of that line, at least one inch of ice accumulated, with locally around 1.5 inches of ice. This resulted in a federal disaster declaration due to widespread tree damage and power outages. Virtually 100 percent of Massac County in the southern tip of the state was without power. The vast majority of nearby Alexander, Pulaski, Pope, Hardin, and southern Johnson Counties lost power. A major 161,000 volt transmission line running across the Ohio River into Kentucky went down. Hundreds of power poles were broken or knocked down in the ice storm area. Power outages generally lasted three to five days in cities and large towns, while many rural areas endured power outages over a week long. Some towns without power lost their water supply because pumps could not fill water towers. A utility company stated the damage was much worse than the ice storm of February 2008, which was their worst on record up until that time. Warming shelters were opened throughout the ice storm area, from Gallatin County southwest across Johnson and Massac Counties to Cairo. Numerous roads were closed by fallen trees and limbs, including State Highway 1 in Hardin County. Blocked roads complicated efforts to restore power and aid storm victims. The U.S. Highway 45 bridge over the Ohio River to Paducah, KY was closed for several days. Some tree limbs fell on cars and houses, puncturing roofs. ||In the heavy sleet and snow area to the north and west, at least two dozen roofs caved in. Most of the collapsed roofs were over large industrial and commercial structures. The roofs of at least three buildings in West City (Franklin County) caved in. The roof of a large metal building caved in and the walls bowed out. Another building that lost its roof was an equipment storage building at a trucking company. The third building was a business. In Harrisburg, the roof of a manufacturing building partially collapsed and damaged a gas line. Another manufacturing building in Marion lost part of its roof, which started a small fire. The building was totalled. The weight of ice and snow caused the roof of the Williamson County Pavilion to bow significantly. The pavilion is used for large public events such as boat shows. The roofs of at least six other buildings in Franklin, Jackson, and Williamson Counties caved in. Falling ice caused damage to guttering and facades of several other buildings. Awnings were damaged. In Carbondale, the windshields and roofs of fourteen cars were crushed by an avalanche of ice and snow from a strip mall. Roof collapses also occurred in Pulaski, Union, Wayne, and Hamilton Counties. In Wayne County, at least eight roof collapses occurred in the Fairfield area alone. Numerous motorists were stranded in snow and sleet, but few accidents were reported. Ambulances had trouble reaching some people who called for help after slipping and falling.||An area of low pressure formed along the Gulf Coast, then tracked slowly east-northeast. High pressure remained anchored west of the Great Lakes, keeping a cold northeast wind flow in place in the lowest levels of the atmosphere. Copious amounts of moisture moved up from the southwest and overrode the cold air in the low levels. This moisture interacted with a couple of disturbances in the upper levels of the atmosphere moving from west to east across the region.

Widespread dense fog developed across far southern Illinois, mainly along and south of a line from Carbondale to Harrisburg. Visibility was below one-quarter mile. Since temperatures were below freezing, light icing occurred in the thick fog. A light south wind flow of milder air over the snow and ice-covered ground set the stage for dense fog.

A storm system tracked from the Texas panhandle northeastward into the Ohio River Valley on January 6th, spreading a mixture of light freezing rain, sleet, and snow across central and southeast Illinois.  Locations along and south of the I-72 corridor experienced the most serious impacts from this system, as a prolonged period of freezing drizzle created icy road conditions and numerous traffic accidents.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Numerous traffic accidents occurred across Champaign County due to icy road conditions.

A few cars were off the road on Interstate 57 near West Frankfort in Franklin County. Multiple accidents occurred in Gallatin County, and the Shawneetown bridge over the Ohio River was impassable. Scattered slick spots were reported on roads throughout the region. A low pressure system skirted just southeast of the region, bringing a wintry mix of precipitation.

A storm system tracked from the Texas panhandle northeastward into the Ohio River Valley on January 6th, spreading a mixture of light freezing rain, sleet, and snow across central and southeast Illinois.  Locations along and south of the I-72 corridor experienced the most serious impacts from this system, as a prolonged period of freezing drizzle created icy road conditions and numerous traffic accidents.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Numerous traffic accidents were reported by Illinois State Police across Vermilion County as a result of icy road conditions.  One woman slid off the road just north of Sidell at around 10:54 AM and was transported to a local hospital.  Another woman lost her life as her vehicle slid off I-74 near mile marker 213 just west of Tilton at 9:30 AM.

A winter storm moved northeast across the Ohio Valley on January 9th and 10th.  Snow developed over northern Illinois during the mid to late evening of January 9th and continued into the late afternoon of January 10th.  Storm total snowfall amounts included 12.0 inches in Evanston, 11.1 inches in Beach Park, 11.0 inches in Des Plaines, 10.9 inches in Lake Bluff, 10.7 inches in Arlington Heights, 10.2 inches at O'Hare Airport, 9.9 inches in Buffalo Grove, 9.6 inches in Northbrook, 9.2 inches in Streamwood, 9.1 inches in Mundelein, 9.0 inches in Rockton, 8.7 inches in Roscoe, 8.7 inches in Fox Lake, 8.6 inches in Carol Stream and 8.2 inches in Wauconda.

After the two heavy snow events of January 9-10 and January 13-14, snow depths ranged from 4 inches at Burlington to 15 inches at Cedar Rapids. Widespread snow depths of 6 to 12 inches were measured across eastern Iowa and northwest Illinois. The deep snow cover set the stage for a strong surge of arctic air that brought record minimum temperatures to the region on January 14-16, 2009. In fact, an all-time record low temperature was established at Cedar Rapids, Iowa on January 15 when the mercury dropped to 29 below. All of eastern Iowa, northwest and west central Illinois and extreme northeast Missouri experienced extreme cold. Actual temperatures were as low as 20 to 40 below north of Highway 34 with 10 to 20 below south of Highway 34, with wind chills to 30 to 50 below.

A frontal boundary draped across eastern Iowa and northern Illinois focused a band of moderate to heavy snow. Widespread accumulations of 5 to 9 inches were common in northeast Iowa and northwest Illinois north of Interstate 80.

A strong clipper storm system moved southeast from the Dakotas to southeast Iowa and then into Illinois on January 13-14, 2009. The path of this area of low pressure brought accumulating snow to portions of eastern Iowa and northwest Illinois. Widespread snow amounts of 6 to 8 inches were noted along and north of the Interstate 80 corridor.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A trained spotter measured 6.3 inches of snow at Mount Carroll.

A 55 year old woman died due to cold exposure on Kolmar Street in Chicago on December 29th.  Low temperatures were in the single digits.  A 58 year old man died from cold exposure in Mount Vernon Park on Aberdeen Street in Chicago on December 31st.  Low temperatures were in the single digits.  A 19 year old man left his home in Braidwood in Will County on the evening of December 30th.  His body was found in a deep ditch about two miles from his home on January 25, 2010.  Low temperatures were in the upper 20s on the morning of December 31st.  A 27 year old man was found dead on the morning of December 28th in Bourbonnais in Kankakee County.  He died from hypothermia as a result of cold exposure.  Low temperatures were in the lower teens.

A complex and slow-moving system brought an extended period of snow to central Illinois from December 25th through December 27th.  The first wave of significant snow occurred during the night of December 25th through midday on the 26th.  Due to favorable temperature and moisture conditions aloft, very large snow flakes were produced, resulting in impressive snowfall rates.  By the time the wave departed the area by the late afternoon of December 26th, a wide swath of 3 to 6 inch snow accumulations had occurred along and west of the Illinois River. A weaker wave brought another period of light snow during the morning of December 27th.  By the time the precipitation ended, all of central Illinois had picked up between 3 and 5 inches of snow, with 7 to 9 inch amounts common along and west of the Illinois River.  Due to the prolonged period of snow, numerous traffic accidents occurred around the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Numerous traffic accidents were reported across Macon County due to snow-covered roadways.  The most significant incident occurred on December 26th at 4:40 PM CST when a vehicle skidded off Illinois 48 near Blue Mound, injuring an 11-year old boy.

Bitterly cold air poured into central Illinois in the wake of a departing storm system.  Thanks to clear skies over a fresh snow cover, early morning temperatures on January 15th and 16th plunged well below zero in much of central and eastern Illinois.  In addition, brisk northwesterly winds created wind-chill values in the 25 below to 40 below zero range.  As a result of the extreme cold, a man in Bloomington-Normal lost his life due to hypothermia.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Low temperatures were 15 below to 20 below zero in much of Champaign County, with wind chills around 30 below zero.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Bitterly cold air poured into central Illinois in the wake of a departing storm system.  Thanks to clear skies over a fresh snow cover, early morning temperatures on January 15th and 16th plunged well below zero in much of central and eastern Illinois.  In addition, brisk northwesterly winds created wind-chill values in the 25 below to 40 below zero range.  As a result of the extreme cold, a man in Bloomington-Normal lost his life due to hypothermia.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Low temperatures were 10 below to 15 below zero in much of Edgar County, with wind chills around 25 below zero.

This accident also injured 2 people.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A powerful low pressure system moved northeast across central Illinois. Damaging southwest winds occurred during the day as solar heating warmed the lower atmosphere. The peak wind gust was measured at 65 mph at the automated observing station at the Carmi airport in White County. Where the January 27 ice storm left weakened and hanging tree limbs, the wind caused another round of power outages. Ice storm power restoration efforts were set back about 12 hours. Large tree limbs were down. In Massac County, a window was blown out of the second story of a house about ten miles north of Metropolis. In Wayne County, the village of Sims was without power after a television antenna toppled onto a power line.

Heavy rain fell across parts of northern Illinois during the evening hours of February 26th which caused flooding and flash flooding across many areas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain fell across much of Dupage County causing flash flooding.  Widespread street flooding was reported.  In Wheaton, Main Street was closed due to flooding between Roosevelt Road and Illinois Street.  In Warrenville, Winfield Road was closed due to flooding between Butterfield and Warrenville Roads.  In Glendale Heights, Bloomingdale Road was closed due to flooding at Army Trail Road.  North of Carol Stream, a sewer cap burst at the corner of Mohican and Pocahantas causing flooding.  In Naperville, a man and child had to be rescued from their swamped vehicle after they tried to drive through floodwaters beneath a railroad overpass at Naper Boulevard.  Around the same time, three vehicles and their occupants became stuck in floodwater in the Elgin, Joliet & Eastern Railway underpass near the intersection of North Aurora and Frontenac roads.  Motorists were also reported stranded in floodwater in the 1000 block of East Gartner Road and Prairie Avenue and Julian Street.  Basement flooding was also reported in Naperville.  Rainfall totals included 2.16 inches in Carol Stream, 2.10 inches near Lisle and 2.02 inches in Oak Brook.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain fell across parts of northern Illinois during the evening hours of February 26th which caused flooding and flash flooding across many areas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain caused flooding across parts of northern Will County late in the evening of February 26th.  Flooding continued through February 28th, especially along creeks, streams and rivers.  Weber Road between Royce and Boughton Roads was closed due to flooding from the East Branch of the Dupage River.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong 500 mb shortwave moved rapidly northeast from the Plains during the day. The shortwave became a closed low over the Great Lakes region by the evening hours. In the lower levels, a strong southwest 850 mb jet from 60 to 65 knots shifted east from the Ozarks across southern Illinois. These features provided sufficient shear and instability for a significant tornado.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Funnel clouds were reported near Mcclure heading toward Union County.
